whereToSee: Heather moorland throughout the Lake District. The fells, Grizedale, the eastern moorlands. Underfoot on most walks.
lakesPopulation: Dominant on acid moorland across the Lakes. You'll walk through it. Berries in late summer.
description: Bilberry is everywhere on the fells. That low shrub with the purple berries. July to September. Pick a few. Leave most. It's the food of grouse and red deer.
identification: Low shrub. Small oval leaves. Purple berries. Smaller than cultivated blueberry.
tips: Late summer for berries. The fells. It's underfoot on most walks. Don't strip it.

seasonalPresence: Flowers June to August. Leaves present year-round.
whereToSee: Boggy moorland. Foulshaw Moss, the fells around Coniston, wet flushes. That yellow spike in summer.
ukPopulation: Widespread in upland and western Britain.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss has good displays. Wet moorland throughout the Lakes. Yellow spikes in June and July.
description: Bog asphodel lights up the wet moorland. Yellow spikes in June and July. Foulshaw Moss. The fells. It likes wet feet. The name ossifragum means bone-breaker. Old belief that sheep eating it got brittle bones. Not true. But the name stuck.
identification: Yellow flower spike. Grass-like leaves. Grows in wet bog. Unmistakeable in flower.
tips: June and July. Foulshaw Moss. Wet moorland. Sunny day for the flowers.

bestTimeOfDay: Any. Sunny days for best display.
whereToSee: Grassland and moorland edge. Foulshaw Moss, the fells, damp grassland. Widespread.
ukPopulation: Widespread in western and northern Britain.
lakesPopulation: Common on damp grassland and moorland edge across the Lakes. Foulshaw Moss has good numbers.
description: Heath spotted orchids are common in the Lakes. Damp grassland. Moorland edge. That spotted leaf. Pink flowers. June and July. Don't pick. They're slow to recover.
identification: Spotted leaves. Pink flowers in a spike. Orchid. Several similar species. The spots on the leaves help.
tips: June and July. Damp grassland. Foulshaw Moss. Don't pick.

bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Dew on the leaves catches the light.
whereToSee: Boggy ground. Foulshaw Moss, wet flushes on the fells, peat bogs. That red sticky leaves.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss has good populations. Wet bog throughout the Lakes. Carnivorous. The sticky leaves catch insects.
description: Sundew is a carnivorous plant. Those red leaves with sticky droplets. They catch insects. Foulshaw Moss. Wet bog. Get down low to see them. Morning dew makes them shine.
identification: Small. Red leaves with sticky droplets. Round-leaved sundew is the common one. Grows in sphagnum.
tips: Foulshaw Moss. Get down low. Morning for the dew. Don't touch. The sticky stuff is the trap.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Berries take two years to ripen.
whereToSee: Limestone areas. The eastern Lake District. Orrest Head, Scout Scar. Also on some acid soils. Declining.
lakesPopulation: Eastern Lakes. Limestone. Scout Scar. Orrest Head. The berries are used for gin. Don't strip the bushes.
description: Juniper is the gin plant. Those blue berries. Two years to ripen. The eastern Lakes. Scout Scar. Orrest Head. It's declining. Grazing and disease. Worth seeking out.
identification: Conifer. Prickly needles. Blue berries. Low shrub or small tree.
tips: Eastern Lakes. Limestone. Scout Scar. The berries. Don't strip them. Leave some for the birds.

seasonalPresence: Flowers March to May. Leaves die back by summer.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. Flowers close in rain and at night.
whereToSee: Ancient woodland. Rannerdale, the Borrowdale woods, Grizedale. That white carpet in spring.
lakesPopulation: Rannerdale is famous for the bluebell and anemone display. Borrowdale woods. Grizedale. Spring. That white carpet.
description: Wood anemones carpet the woodland floor in spring. Rannerdale. Borrowdale. White flowers. They close at night and in rain. March to May. Get there before the canopy closes.
identification: White flowers. Six petals. Actually sepals. Leaves divided. Woodland floor. Spring.
tips: March to May. Rannerdale for the display. Morning. They close in rain.
